notes: Holmes employs Wiggins as the leader of the Baker Street Irregulars — a loose network of street children used for surveillance, tracking, and intelligence-gathering. The relationship is efficient and respectful despite the obvious power asymmetry: Holmes pays fairly (three shillings a day plus a guinea for results), gives clear instructions, and treats Wiggins as a capable operative rather than mere hired help. Wiggins, for his part, delivers reports with self-possession and apparent pride in the work. Holmes recognises that the Irregulars can go where he cannot, and values this access accordingly.
